value: Headline: The Iran war premium is bleeding out of crude. WTI settled at $70.24, down $1.68, while Brent shed $1.93 to $73.57 as Hormuz transit resumed and traders repriced the supply risk that had carried both benchmarks higher. The bearish tape stands in sharp contrast to last week's WPSR, which delivered a hefty 6.1 mb crude draw and a Cushing print firmly below tank-bottom minimums — a structurally tight physical picture now being overwhelmed by geopolitical de-escalation and the prospect of more Iranian barrels. Crude & products: The latest weekly EIA report, for the week ending June 19, 2026, showed commercial crude stocks falling 6.1 mb to 412.1 mb, driven by strong run rates and steady export pull — 4-week crude exports ran 4.93 mb/d against 5.75 mb/d of imports. Refinery utilization eased 0.6 points to 96.1% with crude inputs at 17.11 mb/d, keeping the demand pull intense heading into peak summer. Products diverged: gasoline built 2.1 mb to 216.3 mb and distillate built 3.0 mb to 106.1 mb, with jet up 1.3 mb to 46.3 mb. The standout remains Cushing, where stocks fell another 1.1 mb to 18.96 mb — already operating well below the ~25 mb operational floor, leaving the hub exposed to deliverability stress and basis volatility. Refining margins: Margins held firm even as flat price sank. The 3:2:1 crack sat at $53.49, with the distillate crack the clear leader at $61.30 versus gasoline at $49.58 — a yield signal favoring the diesel barrel despite the 3.0 mb distillate build. Jet cracks lagged at $34.96. RBOB settled at $2.8529/gal (down 5 c) and ULSD at $3.1319/gal (down 8 c), product weakness tracking crude lower but cracks staying wide on resilient summer demand and elevated run rates. Curve structure: WTI remains backwardated, with the M1–M12 spread at $3.25 and the prompt M1–M2 at $0.36 — a curve still pricing near-term tightness consistent with the sub-floor Cushing picture, even as the front rolls lower. Brent backwardation is shallower, M1–M12 at $2.90 and M1–M2 at just $0.12, reflecting the war premium draining fastest off the prompt. The Brent–WTI spread has narrowed to $3.33; that compressed arb dulls the incentive to push US barrels overseas, and softer export pull risks letting domestic crude back up — a downside pressure on WTI unless the spread re-widens. RBOB and ULSD curves remain steeply backward at roughly $19.36 and $19.25/bbl M1–M12, underpinning the front-end product strength. News & geopolitics: The dominant thread is de-escalation: Brent is erasing its Iran war premium as Hormuz flows show signs of recovery, US drillers are adding oil and gas rigs as Hormuz transit resumes, and traders are betting on more Iranian crude returning to market — collectively the catalyst behind yesterday's sell-off. Saudi Arabia is set to slash official selling prices as Hormuz reopens, a bearish signal that Gulf barrels will compete harder into a market where Energy Intelligence flags the spot crude market cracking against sluggish demand. Counter-risks linger: a cargo ship was attacked near Oman, briefly knocking WTI back below $70, and Kazakhstan cut gas output after a drone strike on a Russian processing plant — a reminder that supply-side tail risk hasn't fully cleared. Watch the IAEA's unresolved nuclear puzzle inside Iran and Iraq floating an OPEC exit ahead of EU energy talks in Baghdad as the next directional swing factors; for now, the tape is leaning bearish on barrels coming back, not staying out.
